column3: Upon doing A, B happens / When A, B
column5: 教室に入ると先生がレオ君に言いました。
column6: Upon entering the classroom, the teacher spoke to Leo-kun.
column7: As soon as A happens, B follows. This と is used for a natural, immediate result, not for the speaker's intention or invitation.

column5: お母さんが起こしてくれなかったんです。
column6: Because my mother didn't wake me up.
column7: 〜てくれる means someone does something for me/us. In the negative past, 〜てくれなかった means that helpful action did not happen.

column3: Command telling someone to do something
column7: A firm instruction form often used by parents or teachers. It is softer than a blunt imperative, but still strong.

column4: Verb (ない-stem) + ければなりません
column5: レオ君は早く寝て、早く起きなければなりません
column6: Leo-kun has to go to bed early and get up early.
column7: This pattern means must / have to do something. It is a standard polite way to express obligation or necessity.

column5: レオ君は泣きながら走りました。
column7: This means two actions happen at the same time. The action before ながら is usually the background action.

column3: They did things like singing songs and doing an animal quiz on the bus
column4: Verb (past tense) + り, Verb (past tense) + りする
column5: バスの中で歌を歌ったり、動物クイズをしたりしました
column6: On the bus, they did things like sing songs and do an animal quiz.
column7: Use 〜たり to list examples of actions, not a complete list. It suggests other things may have happened too.

column3: After going to the bathroom, he ended up not knowing the way
column5: トイレに行って道がわからなくなって...
column6: I went to the bathroom and then I couldn't find my way back...
column7: This means to become unable to do something or to no longer be in a state. Here it means the speaker got lost or stopped knowing the way.
